details.gallery_dealer_contact_details_if_applic: West End Gallery, 3/122 High Street, Fremantle WA 6160, contact: Sarah Donnelly, Director, info@westendgallery.com.au, (08) 9335 7821

details_of_proposed_artwork.western_australian_flora_botanical_names: Anigozanthos manglesii (Red and Green Kangaroo Paw), Corymbia calophylla (Marri), Banksia grandis (Bull Banksia), Eucalyptus macrocarpa (Mottlecah), Grevillea preissii (Spider Net Grevillea), Verticordia monadelpha (Ballerina Featherflower)
details_of_proposed_artwork.medium: Mixed media on linen (acrylic, ink, and gold leaf)
details_of_proposed_artwork.approximate_size: Triptych: each panel 90 cm (H) x 60 cm (W) x 3.5 cm (D); overall installed width approx. 190 cm including spacing
details_of_proposed_artwork.description:
Working title: "Breath of the Darling Range".

This proposed work is a mixed-media triptych that celebrates several iconic species of Western Australian flora as they might appear over the course of a single day in late spring in the Darling Range. The three vertical panels will be visually linked but each will represent a different time of day and corresponding mood: dawn, midday, and dusk.

The left panel (dawn) will focus on Anigozanthos manglesii (Red and Green Kangaroo Paw) emerging from shadow, with soft layers of translucent acrylic washes in muted blues and mauves to suggest the cool early morning air. I plan to use fine ink linework to pick out the velvety texture of the flower heads and the angular stems, and subtle gold leaf highlights on dew-dropped edges to catch the light when viewed from an angle. The background will contain ghosted silhouettes of Verticordia monadelpha (Ballerina Featherflower), almost like memories or echoes, hinting at the diversity of the understorey.

The central panel (midday) will be the brightest and most detailed, anchored by the powerful forms of Banksia grandis (Bull Banksia) cones and leaves, with Corymbia calophylla (Marri) blossoms and gumnuts scattered through the composition. Here, I want to convey that dry, almost humming intensity of a midday bushwalk: strong, high-contrast colour, crisp shadows, and layered textural marks. I will build up the surface with thicker acrylic and gel mediums, then carve back into it with scratching and sanding to reveal underlayers, mimicking the rough, scarred bark and the layered history of the bush. Tiny ink details will show insect nibbling on leaves and the irregularities that make each plant individual.

The right panel (dusk) will feature Eucalyptus macrocarpa (Mottlecah) and Grevillea preissii (Spider Net Grevillea), with their rich reds and silvery foliage emerging from a deep, moody ground. The palette will shift toward indigo, burgundy, and charcoal, with metallic glazes to evoke the last sideways light catching on dusty leaves and seed pods. This panel will have a slightly more abstracted treatment, with some forms dissolving into gestural marks to suggest the way colours and shapes blur as light fades. I want viewers to feel that quiet, contemplative moment when the bush settles and the sounds soften.

Conceptually, the triptych is about noticing the "micro-dramas" of Western Australian flora that we often walk past without really seeing. I grew up in the Perth Hills, and some of my earliest memories are of my grandfather pointing out the different banksias and marri blossoms on family walks, explaining which ones the birds favoured and how to spot new growth after a fire. This piece is a way of honouring that lived experience and the deep connection many Western Australians feel with our local bush. I intend to spend time sketching on-site in the Darling Range and referencing my own photographs taken over the last few wildflower seasons, rather than relying solely on stock images, to keep the work grounded in specific places I know well.

From a practical standpoint, the work is designed to be flexible for exhibition: each panel is on a 90 x 60 cm stretched linen, professionally primed and ready to hang with D-rings and wire. The triptych can be installed tightly together for a more unified mural-like effect, or with 5–10 cm spacing to allow each time-of-day panel to breathe. The mixed-media approach (acrylic, ink, and gold leaf) is chosen for its durability and lightfastness, making the work suitable for long-term display in a gallery or public-facing interior environment. Varnishing with a semi-matte UV-protective varnish will ensure the richer pigments and metallic details are preserved while minimising glare.

Overall, "Breath of the Darling Range" aims to be both scientifically respectful (through accurate botanical structures and correct depiction of key features such as banksia cones, marri blossoms, and kangaroo paw flower forms) and emotionally evocative, inviting viewers to slow down and recognise the complexity and resilience of Western Australian flora in their everyday lives.
